fact: June 14 is exactly 165 days into the year, which means there are exactly 200 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when strawberry plants are typically bursting with the juiciest, reddest berries of the entire year.
The sun rises before 5:30 AM in most northern cities on June 14, making it one of the earliest sunrise days ever.
Fireflies usually start their crazy blinking light shows around this date in North America, turning backyards into natural dance parties.
June 14 falls in the middle of Gemini season in the zodiac calendar.
In Antarctica, June 14 brings some of the coldest, darkest days of winter with temperatures that can freeze your spit before it hits the ground.
Dandelions have typically transformed into those white puff balls by this date, ready to spread their seeds everywhere when you blow on them.
The Northern Hemisphere is tilted so far toward the sun on June 14 that some places in Alaska and northern Canada get sunlight for over 19 hours straight.
June 14 sits just one week before the summer solstice, making it one of the longest days of the year for anyone living north of the equator.
Mosquitoes often reach peak annoyance levels around this date in many temperate regions because the warm, humid weather creates perfect breeding conditions.
In the Southern Hemisphere, June 14 typically brings crisp autumn air and trees dropping their colorful leaves like natural confetti.
The moon phase on June 14 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it appears extra low and orange in northern skies.
